author: @selfcorrected-Bobby
text: i just had an idea. you know looms and spinsters wheels have those footpedals to keep them running. I wonder if you could make use of such a foot powered device to power a HEPA filter for doing sterile labwork in front of. I see you say they use the carbon filter in aircons or whatever, but squirrel cage blowers are pricey and tke a lot of electricity, ad can blow out / fuse. Would be SO cool to be able to have a foot powerd laminar flow desk where sterile air is blowing over the workspace in the lab to do sterile mushroom tissue culture where the footpedal through some mechanism creates constant wind pressure through the HEPA filter for sterile airflow on the desk, i.e. laminar flow hood for sterile tissue culture in a laboratory. Also I wonder if it's possible to make a High Efficiency Particular Air filter at home with reticulated carbon foam or something? The problem is the micrometer size at which or through which particles are let through, to prevent contams landing on the er, shroom myceium? The idea is to obtain a pure tissue culture to propagate the dears.  You could have two pedals so you are like basically exercising while powering your laminar flow desk, instead of wasting a ton of electricity and also having to replac a squirrel cage blower, as that HEPA filter requires a strong current of air to force a consistent flow of sterile air through the high efficiency particulate air filter, so would love to know..

author: @greenaum
text:
Just noticing from the scientific paper at the beginning, on the printed paper battery, it has almost no currrent capacity, very little power. Not gonna be enough to power an LED you could see by, or even barely an LED at all. Not in anything pocket-sized at least. Water-activated batteries have been around for decades, a shop in Blackpool was selling them 30 years ago as a cheap novelty, water-"powered" watch. There's nothing special about them, their sole advantage seems to be a longer shelf-life than ordinary batteries, no self-discharge til you activate them. 

Powering an LCD alarm clock is a dead giveaway, they use essentially no power at all. Like solar-powered calculators, try powering your fridge from that solar panel! I can't think of *anything* that'd work from one of those tiny solar panels. That's the scale of the printed water-activated battery in the paper at the start.

author: @falls2shine712
text:
Thanks, that's a great one to know about.
Have you seen anything on John Worrell Keely? A 19th century inventor.
Apparently he found a way to infinitely ramp up negative pressure, by using vacuum chamber ratios of 3:6:9 to create a resonant effect.
Something along those lines.
Dale Pond gave a pretty good explanation of the idea, if you search that name on youtube.
It might inspire a few projects.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: Thank you for making this video. What are the half cell reactions for this battery? I looked up the paper, but it doesn't show the reactions, it just lists the ingredients: Zinc Anode, Graphite Cathode. I find it odd that a scientific paper doesn't show the equations...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it won't be neat mate but the basic reaction is going to be zinc to zinc hydroxide, then oxide then chloride probably - though i would guess a hypochlorite as an intermediate and probably hydrogen on the other side

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkeringApologies... Looking more carefully I realize this is just a Zinc-Air battery... with (dried) paper thrown in to hold the electrolyte in place. The water is used to activate it. Sigh...

What voltage did it produce? This is actually quite important as the current figure doesn't say much without it...200 milliamps at 1.5 volts is not 200 milliamps at 12 volts etc.

replies.author: @DistracticusPrime
replies.text:
I love this idea of yours! 
I once lived in an old house with problematic septic system. The full tank would back up into the bathtub (not so bad) but also leak around the toilet drain wax ring onto the floor (so bad). Of course the previous owners put carpet! So I bought a water detector alarm and put it behind the toilet. It would sound like a smoke alarm if the carpet got wet. It served us well until, ironically, the battery died.

replies.author: @sjsomething4936
replies.text: @szogun1987generally speaking it’d be superior for sure, but the batteries in the battery bank will degrade over time, even when not used. This specifically won’t really do that if well sealed from moisture for years, perhaps even decades.
